title: Spaghetti alla gricia
subtitle: Rome's oldest pasta, guanciale and pecorino without the egg
excerpt: Gricia is carbonara's ancestor: guanciale rendered until the fat runs clear, pasta water worked into a sauce with aged pecorino, no egg yolks. The result is richer and more mineral than carbonara, and more forgiving of timing errors.
sourceNotes: Gricia is a Roman preparation documented in Pellegrino Artusi, La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene (1891, public domain), though Artusi's Lazio chapter focuses on amatriciana. Regional food historians trace gricia to the Apennine hill town of Grisciano, near Amatrice, where cured pig cheek and aged sheep's-milk cheese have been paired since at least the eighteenth century.

content.text: Gricia predates carbonara by at least a century. Where carbonara needs egg yolks and careful temperature control, gricia relies on two ingredients: cured pig's cheek and aged sheep's-milk cheese. The technique is the same emulsification trick, but pasta water and fat do the work without the risk of scrambled eggs.

. It should render slowly in its own fat, not fry at high heat. You want the fat translucent and the meat golden, not brown or crisp. The rendered fat becomes part of the sauce, and burnt guanciale will dominate every forkful.

Use spaghetti or rigatoni. Roman trattorias often serve gricia with rigatoni because the ridges hold the sauce, but spaghetti is the traditional choice in the Lazio hill towns where the dish originated. The pasta water must be generously salted; pecorino adds no salt to the sauce itself, so the water carries the seasoning.

items.name: Guanciale
items.prepNote: cut into lardons roughly 1 cm thick

content.text: A 26 cm frying pan is large enough for the sauce; the pasta finishes in it. You need a microplane or the finest setting on a box grater for the pecorino: coarser grating leaves the cheese granular rather than silky.

Put the guanciale in a cold frying pan. Set over medium-low heat. Let it render slowly, turning occasionally, for 12 to 15 minutes until the fat is clear and the meat is golden at the edges. The pan should sizzle gently, not spit. Remove the pan from the heat. Leave the guanciale and its fat in the pan.

Bring a large pan of well-salted water to a rolling boil. Add the {{pasta-dried}} of pasta and cook until 2 minutes short of the packet's al dente time. Scoop out 200 ml of pasta water before draining.

Return the guanciale pan to medium heat. Add the drained pasta and 100 ml of the reserved pasta water. Toss vigorously for 1 to 2 minutes until the liquid reduces and coats the pasta. Take the pan off the heat. Add the {{pecorino-romano}} of pecorino and the black pepper. Toss again, adding splashes of pasta water (a tablespoon at a time) to reach a loose, glossy sauce that clings without pooling. The residual heat melts the cheese; you do not want the pan back on the flame or the cheese will seize.
Divide between warm bowls immediately. Add an extra grind of pepper at the table.

items.fix: Pull the pan off the heat entirely before adding cheese; the pasta's residual temperature is enough
items.cause: Pecorino added while the pan was still on a hot burner, causing the cheese to seize and split
items.symptom: Sauce is grainy rather than smooth

items.fix: Add pasta water a tablespoon at a time and toss; the sauce should flow loosely
items.cause: Not enough pasta water added, or the pasta was overcooked before going into the pan
items.symptom: Sauce is too thick and gluey

items.fix: Start in a cold pan over medium-low heat; patience at this stage determines the texture
items.cause: Heat too high during rendering, causing the meat to fry and toughen before the fat runs
items.symptom: Guanciale is chewy rather than silky

items.fix: Taste the pasta water before cooking. It should taste distinctly salty, like a light brine
items.cause: Under-salted pasta water; pecorino adds no salt to the sauce

content.text: Gricia is the oldest of Rome's four canonical pasta dishes. Carbonara, amatriciana, and cacio e pepe are its descendants or relatives. The name almost certainly comes from Grisciano, a hamlet in the Rieti valley near Amatrice. Shepherds moving flocks between summer and winter pastures carried guanciale and pecorino as keepable provisions. The dish has no tomato, no egg, and no fuss. Roman restaurants serve it without ceremony. It asks for nothing more than good ingredients and a steady hand with the pasta water.
